item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The one thing I don't understand is the containment procedures - it seems like the only anomalous property is the casefile swap between universes, so why does it matter if the disc goes above -210C?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get it. Why would another universe decide to hide artifacts? They're essentially magic, so I can't understand why any alternate universe version of the SPC would cover it up. Are they evil?

I do like the subversion of Syndicate of Crab Punishers' name though. No uptally from me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems their ideals of hiding information are different from the SCP's. SCP is very secretive, and compartmentalizes everything. Even the SCP's first response teams get little information about what SCP they are dealing with before more specialized SCPs show up. (At least according SCP-096's file.)

This is because part of their mission is hiding this stuff from the public, so redacting stuff makes it so that even if some information gets out, it reduces the amount of breadcrumbs that would lead to more info about the SCP.

The SPC obviously have no need to hide the anomalous (although I suppose for them it's normal.) so they don't have any redactions, apart from k-t-r and the like. Also, the statement at the bottom shows that their culture may be adverse to hiding information.

Last but not least, they don't appear to be hostile, so even if the SCP has guns trained on them, they don't see the SCP as an enemy, because if they don't show hostility, the SCP won't act, so they don't care if the SCP knows stuff about them.

item.title: Re: It's called the tags
item.content:encoded: I think the previous poster meant an in-universe classification system for the Foundation, since tags are IRL and not in-universe.

item.title: Well, at least we know one GOI...
item.content:encoded: The Send Crabs to Purgatory organization.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: This image originates from here, which has not indicated that its content is released under CC 3.0. As such, it is assumed to be non-compliant with the license and has since been removed.

item.title: the Order of the Sacred Button-Pushers
item.content:encoded: We are the Order of the Sacred Button-Pushers and we Will press all buttons

item.title: 1764 (and other numbers)
item.content:encoded: SPC-1764—interesting number. The NCC-1764 USS Defiant is a Federation starship featured in "In a Mirror, Darkly", a Mirror-verse episode where said starship somehow ended up in an alternate version of the 22nd century. Other than wild speculation, SCP-343 (God) is also an interesting number: it equals 7 * 7 * 7; the number seven is considered a lucky number and 777 is considered the antithesis of 666.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. It shows the perspective of a similar but different organization in an alternate universe and how they view the SCP Foundation. It's very interesting seeing the differences, and thus earns my upvote.

On a side note, I also like the classification system that the DARD uses better that the S-E-K classification that the SCP Foundation uses. It's just more detailed, and I wish we'd use a more detailed classification system.

item.title: Wild Mass Guessing
item.content:encoded:
Notice: The following content may contain wild misunderstandings of quantum-mechanical theory.

  • The object (and both documents describing it) exists in an anomalous superposition that does not cause a wavefunction collapse upon observation.
  • Additionally, the modification of the document violates the no-communication theorem (i.e. superluminal communication via quantum entanglement is impossible)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only lament is that I cannot see the Foundation version of the document (which, as explained, swapped places with the DARD counterpart).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Th-m—l

Shouldn't it be
TH??M??L?
Two letters between the H and the M, "thAUmiel" if I remember it right?
Edit: Fuckin' wikidot strikethroughs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i want to read more dard files

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took me a moment to get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: HNG why cant we upvote comments

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I expected sharks. I am dissapoint.
JK, have your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, hold on. How did… how did either organization even create a containment document in the first place if any changes get reverted to the other side's version?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Well. That's a literary concession. If the DARD were being smart, their entire containment procedures set would be a big copy of Hello.jpg (goatse)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I have enjoyed the pugilistic SPC, I think it's clear that the joke is getting a bit stale.
This is a wonderful and well-executed alternative back-ronym and I hope we see as much done with it as with the shark sluggers.

Random Musings: For an organization supposedly savvy to the ways of information warfare, the SPC is not showing very good counter-intelligence practices. They know that the SCP will be reading whatever they write, and yet they continue to give paragraph after paragraph with clues to their organization, capabilities, and even a neat summary of what their analysts think of the potential enemy.

This is like briefing Nixon on how the soviets managed to bug the oval office, in the oval office, and never actually removing the bug.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh shit. This is excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read that as "Second Coming Out".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, oh, that's clever. The best part is their analysis of us and our format. This is so meta it hurts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done! Upvoted without regrets and I love the "Thaumiel" thing hidden in there!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your puns won't make you the center of attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who thinks this comes from a world slightly different from 093?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, that was super meta. When it dawned on me that I was reading their version of the "containment procedures", I immediately decided to up vote it.
Makes me wonder how the SCP plans to contain it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Come to think of it, I'm not entirely sure 055 isn't not a shark…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bravo, sir! This makes we curious about the rest of their world-how SPCs are utilized and how other groups of interest fit in (are MC&D respectable businessmen? Does Wondertainment own a bunch of toy stores?). Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmmm, it's okay. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did like the classification system as well. Is this article what tips us towards actually changing ours…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the alternate universe's casefile refers to the object in question by its casefile number, indicating that Applied Nomenclature is not yet a well-understood field of study in the alternative universe.

This made me happy and I pressed a button accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love your comment almost as much as the article itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're joking, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who's ever been attacked by the absence of a shark?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is possibly my favorite format screw on the site.

(If you don't think it's that much of a format screw - well. Depends on what the format is supposed to be and what universe you're in, I suspect!)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun fact: that was the precise point at which I went "…. wait. I bet Clef wrote this."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel a multi-universal constant would be "For the love of , please use the sandbox"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At least until the second Coming, Sorts.

I'll just let myself out, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Addendum: An unauthorized attempt to copy the main file led to the creation of an additional anomalous document consisting of 500 pages detailing the various ways that the object can and cannot be used to subdue aquatic specimens of the Selachimorpha superorder. The significance of the document is unknown at this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A good SPC article not about brawling with selachimorphs? Glory be to Anat-in-Vigor!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any alt-Foundation that wanted to be friendly to its alts would be a pretty lousy Foundation. Every alt is an anomaly to their own standard which must be secured and contained.

It's jerks all the way down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is brilliant. +1

Reminds me of that one article written up in numerous styles from numerous multiversal Foundation analogues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the Foundation ever going to get along with any of it's alternate universe counterparts?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this is meta. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral Vote. Its a great article, and shows what might have been had we not gone scientific and mechanical as a global society. That alone would get my upvote. But its soooooooo wordy! Too much black space!

Edit: original comment was in 2013. It's now 2017, and I had to upvote it on this read through :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this was just cute until I got to this part:

In addition, the use of the term "K-t-r" as an object's classification indicates that the alternative organization feels safe in using a kabballistic term of power in a poetic sense

It got even better from there. Good idea, written well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for being overall interesting and using a classification system that isn't pointless fluff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quite the mindfuck, since even when I've "got it", I'm still not sure if I actually did.

From what I can tell, this disk had some other anomalous property, but the SPC, for one reason or another, has considered it important to hide it from the staff who would read this. Then, the property of the SCP document bleeding into their world (and vice versa) was noted after the document was written up.

And if that's true I absolutely love it. It can either be as dangerous as it implies, something "dangerous" to them only (heresy to the emperor), or related to their view of redaction as a form of "information warfare". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to imagine how their universe would critique articles.

It's SPC, not SCP. Come on, it's right there in the URL and plastered everywhere on the site. We're not the Syndicate of Chondrichthye Punchers.

What's with the black lines? Is that part of the anomaly or something?

No one should have to check with an alchemist. Why would someone who doesn't know alchemy be assigned to this thing in the first place? That's like someone who isn't proficient in necromancy being assigned to SPC-816.

Why is such a powerful object just being locked up? We're not DARD.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Great read. The addendum was slightly too heavy-handed for me, but not enough to detract from a solid upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had to read it twice to finally understand what was going on, it was worth the effort +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I understood what was happening, and then saw 'SPC', I thought Djoric had main-listed an ironic article regarding sharks. From that I assumed I was in for a treat, because if it was good enough to be a -j surviving on the main list, it must be pretty freaking amazing.

With that expectation in mind, I was pretty disappointed. Just kidding mostly, I liked it.

Neutral vote in all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm being finicky, but overall I felt the article lacked punch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you did there.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oof. Metaversal Merge Scenarios can be nightmarish.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sometimes the absence of sharks is more dangerous than actual sharks.

Are We C—l Yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn good work. It took me a minute to realize that this was "our" version of the document due to the anomaly. And I'll agree that this is one of the best format screws I've experienced.

+1. Good show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely an interesting take on the format, and one of the better alternate artifact documentation schemes I've read.

One wonders if the Foundation really is fucking things up by using the item number and the Keter object class so freely, or if conditions are such that it would only be a problem in the SPCverse.
